quiz.name: Session 08 Final Practice Quiz: Event Correlation and Normalization
quiz.intro: 5 short-answer questions (10 pts each) + 2 long-answer questions (25 pts each) = 100 pts. Time: 30 minutes.

questiontext.text: (10 points) A CEF log contains cs1=admin_policy with cs1Label=PolicyName. Why does CEF use cs1/cs1Label pairs instead of naming the field PolicyName directly? What is the normalization drawback?

graderinfo.text: Award full marks for answers that: - Explain CEF's design goal: generic format usable by any vendor without predefined field names (5 pts) - Identify the normalization problem: two vendors can use cs1 for different data; normalization pipeline must know vendor identity to correctly interpret cs1 (5 pts)

questiontext.text: (10 points) A Logstash filter tries two grok patterns for a log message. The message matches neither and gets tagged _grokparsefailure. List two actions you should take and explain why each is important.

graderinfo.text: Award 5 pts per correct action with explanation: 1. Monitor failure rate as a pipeline health metric — sudden spikes indicate upstream format changes that silently break detection 2. Investigate failing events and add/fix patterns — parse failures mean events cannot be searched by field; store them as-is for retroactive processing

questiontext.text: (10 points) Explain the difference between a sliding window and a tumbling window for correlation. Give one use case where a sliding window is more appropriate.

graderinfo.text: Award marks for: - Tumbling: fixed non-overlapping intervals; events evaluated once per interval (3 pts) - Sliding: re-evaluates as each event arrives; any span of defined duration is evaluated (3 pts) - Use case: brute force detection where attacker deliberately straddles window boundaries to evade tumbling window threshold (4 pts)

question.name: Q4 (Short): Password Spray vs Brute Force Detection

questiontext.text: (10 points) What is the ATT&CK sub-technique for password spraying (trying common passwords across many accounts)? Why does this distinction from traditional brute force matter for detection rule design?

graderinfo.text: Award marks for: - Correct technique: T1110.003 Password Spraying (3 pts) - Traditional brute force detected by counting failures per user.name (2 pts) - Password spray detected by counting DISTINCT user.name values per source.ip (3 pts) - Wrong grouping means wrong detection: per-user threshold misses spray entirely (2 pts)

questiontext.text: (10 points) An analyst proposes whitelisting the entire 10.0.0.0/8 internal range from authentication-failure alerts since "real attacks come from the internet." What is wrong with this reasoning? Give two specific attack scenarios it would miss.

graderinfo.text: Award marks for: - Insider threats operate from internal IPs (5 pts) - Post-initial-access lateral movement: attacker inside network uses internal IPs to attack other hosts — this is exactly when detection is most critical (5 pts) - Partial credit for any other valid internal-origin attack scenario

question.name: Q6 (Long): Detection Engineering for Scheduled Task

questiontext.text:
(25 points) You are designing a Sigma rule for T1053.005 — Scheduled Task Creation.
Describe: (1) which data sources you would use and why, (2) what specific fields/patterns form the detection logic, (3) at least three legitimate false positive use cases and how to handle each, (4) ATT&CK tags and severity level with justification.

graderinfo.text: Scoring rubric: 1. Data sources (6 pts): Windows Security EID 4698 (primary - task XML, user, name), EID 4702 (updates), Sysmon EID 1 for schtasks.exe command line. Award 2 pts per source with rationale. 2. Detection logic (7 pts): Specific fields - task name, subject user, task command content (PowerShell/cmd), trigger type (onlogon/onstart/minute). Sigma rule structure with filter for \Microsoft\Windows\ paths. 3. False positives (6 pts): Windows Update / Defender, software installers, IT automation (SCCM/Ansible). 2 pts per FP scenario with concrete handling approach. 4. ATT&CK tags (3 pts): attack.persistence, attack.t1053.005, optionally attack.privilege_escalation. Severity Medium with rationale (attacker already has code exec, persistence is follow-on). Escalation conditions. 5. Overall quality (3 pts): completeness, engineering mindset, practical applicability.

questiontext.text:
(25 points) Your SOC has these coverage gaps for ransomware techniques: T1078 (Valid Accounts) has threshold-only coverage; T1059.001 (PowerShell) has no coverage; T1490 (VSS Deletion) is fully covered; T1486 (encryption) has partial EDR coverage.
Explain why each gap matters for ransomware detection, and design a 3-rule detection chain that enables detection before the encryption stage (T1486).

graderinfo.text: Scoring rubric: 1. Gap analysis (8 pts): - T1078 gap: threshold misses credential theft without brute force (modern ransomware uses bought credentials) - 4 pts - T1059.001 gap: PowerShell is the primary staging/execution mechanism; missing it means entire execution phase is blind - 4 pts 2. Three-rule chain (12 pts - 4 pts each): - Rule 1: Behavioral T1078 detection - login from new country/location not in user baseline - Rule 2: PowerShell download cradle detection (DownloadString, IEX, etc.) - high confidence - Rule 3: Sequence rule - discovery tools followed by VSS deletion on same/adjacent host Each rule: correct trigger logic, appropriate severity, and why it catches ransomware before T1486. 3. Chain explanation (5 pts): explains how the three rules provide 3 independent detection opportunities; identifies that detection before T1486 is the only chance to prevent data loss; includes timing analysis (Rule 1 catches access, Rule 2 catches staging, Rule 3 catches 15-30min pre-deployment window).
